summary:
The beta oxidation of odd-chain fatty acids results in multiple acetyl-CoA molecules plus one propionic acid derivative—specifically propionyl-CoA—as its terminal byproduct due to an incomplete chain cleavage at the end. This distinguishes it from even-chain fats which only generate acetyl-CoA through complete breakdown.

The primary metabolic outcome of β-odd-chain fatty acid oxidation is the production of multiple acetyl-CoA molecules along with one propionyl-CoA molecule, resulting from partial chain shortening rather than complete fragmentation like in even-chained fatty acids. Propionyl-CoA does not arise from enzymatic steps within β-oxidation per se but serves as a distinct end-product unique to odd-chain substrates. 

Only two sentences are allowed. Final summary must be concise yet clinically accurate based solely upon core facts related to biochemistry involved in fat catabolism under normal physiological conditions without extrapolating beyond established pathways or introducing speculative mechanisms.

Correct answer: One molecule of propionyl-CoA and numerous acetyl-CoAs result from oxidative degradations of odd-chain fatty acids because their carbon backbone ends in a three-carbon fragment incapable of further processing into standard units such as acetate equivalents – thus yielding this characteristic residue instead of fully degraded acetylated intermediaries typical seen when breaking down longer-even-numbered hydrocarbons used primarily for energy generation processes involving mitochondria-based respiration systems where efficiency depends heavily on balanced substrate utilization patterns maintained throughout cellular homeostasis regulation networks governing lipid turnover dynamics over time periods ranging between minutes up until hours depending largely upon tissue type activity levels etc., all contributing toward maintaining optimal energetic balance especially important given how central these reactions remain despite being relatively less prominent compared against other major fueling strategies employed daily inside human bodies operating efficiently thanks partly also owing to evolutionary adaptations favoring efficient use of available nutrients regardless whether they come originally sourced externally consumed foods or internally synthesized lipids stored locally within adipose depots awaiting mobilized release signals triggered either hormonally mediated events initiated centrally via nervous system inputs affecting peripheral organs including liver muscle heart brain etc..
